results.label: Confident — GDPR hiring AI, EU
results.input: We are building an AI tool that screens job applicants and ranks them by suitability. We operate in Germany and France.

domains.reasoning: Applicant screening necessarily processes personal data (privacy) and uses AI/ML to rank candidates (ai).

jurisdictions.reasoning: Operations explicitly stated in Germany and France, both EU member states.

data_types.reasoning: Applicant screening processes behavioural data (suitability signals) and general personal information (CV, contact details, etc.).

sector.reasoning: Tool is explicitly designed for job applicant screening and ranking.

results.label: Confident — US health app
results.input: Our iOS app lets patients in the United States track chronic conditions, log medications, and share data with their doctors.

domains.reasoning: Privacy is certain (personal health data processed); AI flagged with medium confidence as the description lacks explicit mention of algorithmic decision-making, but health tracking apps commonly employ predictive or analytical ML.

jurisdictions.reasoning: Explicitly stated as United States; HIPAA and related federal health privacy laws apply to patient health data handling.

data_types.reasoning: App explicitly processes chronic condition data, medications, and patient identifiers shared with doctors—all health data; general_pi included as patient identity and account information are necessarily captured.

sector.reasoning: App is explicitly designed for patients to manage health conditions and interact with physicians.

results.input: We provide algorithmic credit scoring to UK banks. Our model uses transaction history and behavioural signals to approve or decline loan applications.

results.label: Confident — children's education platform, US
results.input: We run an online learning platform for K-12 students in the United States. We collect names, grades, usage data, and video recordings of tutoring sessions.

domains.reasoning: The platform collects personal data (names, grades, usage data, video recordings) from identified K-12 students.

jurisdictions.reasoning: K-12 students in the United States are explicitly stated; FERPA (federal) and state student privacy laws (e.g., COPPA for younger students) apply.

data_types.reasoning: K-12 students are minors (children); names and grades are general_pi; video recordings of tutoring sessions constitute communications data.

sector.reasoning: K-12 online learning platform is explicitly an education sector service.

results.label: Confident — EU smart city surveillance
results.input: Our computer vision platform processes CCTV footage across public spaces in Amsterdam and Brussels to monitor crowd density and detect incidents in real time.

domains.reasoning: Computer vision processes personal data (faces, movement patterns in CCTV footage), uses AI/ML algorithms, and involves networked camera infrastructure with real-time data processing.

jurisdictions.reasoning: Deployment explicitly stated in Amsterdam (Netherlands) and Brussels (Belgium), both EU member states subject to GDPR.

data_types.reasoning: CCTV footage captures faces (biometric), movement/positioning in public spaces (location), and crowd behavior patterns (behavioural).

sector.reasoning: Public space CCTV monitoring for crowd density and incident detection is typically operated by municipal or government authorities, though private security deployment is possible; government is the most likely use case.
